And obviously that's become one of the biggest things out of Coinbase. Do you have a similar story where there was an idea that you initially weren't so hot on, but ended up being...
turns.zh: Brian Armstrong 在这里谈到，当 USDC 的想法被提给他时，他说：“啊，我不确定。听起来有点傻，但好吧，如果你们想做，那就去做吧。”显然这成了 Coinbase 最大的事情之一。你有没有类似的故事，一个你最初不太热衷的想法，但最终却……

turns.en: Oh gosh, there's probably so many. I mean, streaks are one. I did not like the idea of streaks. Did not want to do it. I actually fought very hard to kill streaks over the years. We've always been anti-metric at Snap. Like no metrics, no thank you.
turns.zh: 哦天哪，可能有很多。比如 streaks 就是一个。我不喜欢 streaks 这个想法。不想做。实际上多年来我一直努力想取消 streaks。Snap 一直反对指标。没有指标，谢谢。

turns.en: There metrics or gamification? You mean like user facing...
turns.zh: 是指指标还是游戏化？你是说面向用户的……

turns.en: Just metric? Like there's almost no metrics in Snapchat. It's hard to find...
turns.zh: 就是指标？Snapchat 里几乎没有指标。很难找到……

turns.en: Internally or externally?
turns.zh: 内部还是外部？

turns.en: For the user-facing metric. Like most social services: how many friends do you have? How many followers do you have? How many likes do you have? Comment like everything has...
turns.zh: 面向用户的指标。比如大多数社交服务：你有多少朋友？你有多少粉丝？你有多少赞？评论等等，一切都有……

turns.en: You want their experience to be one of numbers.
turns.zh: 你希望他们的体验是数字化的。

turns.en: Look at a bunch of numbers. You know, we want to get out of the way and help you communicate with your friends. And so I've been over the years just like trying to get rid of streaks. And what really changed for me was learning how much people love streaks as an opportunity to connect with one another. And so over time I would just hear these stories, people again evanap.com people would send these emails of like 'You wouldn't believe it. I moved halfway across the world. I was separated from my best friend. I got a divorce. I got a new job. And every single day I stayed in touch with my best friend. And that changed my life and was so powerful for me.' And I was like, 'Wow, like okay.' But just sometimes having an invitation to communicate or an invitation to connect is so important. And so I think over time I've come around to really seeing the value there.
turns.zh: 看一堆数字。你知道，我们想不碍事，帮助你与朋友交流。所以多年来我一直试图取消 streaks。真正改变我的是了解到人们多么热爱 streaks 作为彼此联系的机会。随着时间的推移，我听到这些故事，人们通过 evanap.com 发邮件说：“你简直不敢相信。我搬到了地球另一端，与最好的朋友分离。我离婚了，换了新工作。但每天我都和最好的朋友保持联系。这改变了我的生活，对我意义重大。”我就想：“哇，好吧。”但有时仅仅是一个沟通的邀请或联系的邀请就如此重要。所以我认为随着时间的推移，我逐渐看到了其中的价值。

turns.en: Speaks to the digital equivalent of getting out of the house. It's just it's important to get out.
turns.zh: 这相当于数字版的“出门”。出门很重要。

turns.en: Sometimes you got to get out of... and you got to do that online as well. Okay. That's interesting.
turns.zh: 有时你必须走出去……在线也要这样做。好吧，这很有趣。

turns.en: And then when we talk about the product design process, are designers kind of in the driver's seat of this similar? I don't know if you've compared notes with Brian at Airbnb where they have like a very design forward culture.
turns.zh: 那么当我们谈论产品设计流程时，设计师是否类似地处于主导地位？我不知道你是否和 Airbnb 的 Brian 交流过，他们那里有非常注重设计的文化。

turns.en: I think design is really important. We have a very design-forward culture. Designers are deeply involved in product decisions. They often drive the creative process, and we encourage them to take ownership of features they're passionate about.
turns.zh: 我认为设计非常重要。我们有一种非常注重设计的文化。设计师深度参与产品决策。他们经常推动创意过程，我们鼓励他们对自己热衷的功能负责。

ts.sec: Snap's culture and Norway's early traction

turns.en: Is that how the culture at Snap is?
turns.zh: Snap 的文化是这样的吗？

turns.en: You know, I'd say that culture at Snap is like a thoughtfully managed dialogue between design and engineering and product management as well, data science, you name it. Because for us, the business was always built initially on the dialogue between myself and Bobby, right? He has a heavier engineering background. I was more of a design background, but he really loved design and I actually loved engineering, so we were able to really debate things and see things from different angles. That was always something that really helped the business grow. We tried to model that as a team where it's not necessarily design-led or product-led or engineering-led; it's a healthy dialogue and relationship between all these really important functions in our business.
turns.zh: 我觉得 Snap 的文化是设计、工程、产品管理、数据科学等各个职能之间经过深思熟虑的对话。因为对我们来说，业务从一开始就是建立在我和 Bobby 的对话之上的，对吧？他工程背景更强，我偏设计，但他真的很爱设计，而我也很喜欢工程，所以我们能真正地辩论，从不同角度看问题。这始终是帮助业务成长的关键。我们试图以此塑造团队文化——不一定由设计、产品或工程主导，而是所有重要职能之间健康的对话和关系。

turns.en: When you talk about data science, one thing I'm reminded of is often in social networks because of the network effects, you get these very strong country network effects where like Orkut was a Google social network that no one really used for a long time except all of Brazil, who were really into it. So you get these funny regional effects. Are there any countries that are just really disproportionately wildly into Snap?
turns.zh: 说到数据科学，我想起社交网络常因网络效应产生很强的国家性网络效应，比如 Orkut 是谷歌的社交网络，除了全巴西人很热衷之外，其他地区很久都没人用。所以会有这种有趣的地域效应。有没有哪个国家对 Snap 的喜爱特别不成比例地狂热？

turns.en: Well, Norway was the first place that Snapchat got traction. I mean, we had no traction anywhere, not the US. We had no traction anywhere except Norway. In fact, in the very early days,
turns.zh: 嗯，挪威是 Snapchat 第一个获得用户的地方。我们当时在任何地方都没有起色，包括美国，只有挪威有。实际上在早期，

turns.en: I did not know this.
turns.zh: 这我还真不知道。

turns.en: Oh, it was a blast. In the early days, I think this was after we had gotten venture funding, maybe after we had gotten our first round. We'd raised $400,000, something like that. And we were really growing in Norway, big time growing in Norway. Like a Canadian girlfriend, you know, my app is big in Norway, trust me. So we literally got on an airplane, flew to Norway, and we were famous. I mean, it was unbelievable. You were in a 7-Eleven. People are like, "What? You guys work at Snapchat? That's crazy." And I remember just being like, "Oh gosh, I hope this happens in more places." It was an amazing experience.
turns.zh: 哦，那太棒了。早期，大概是我们拿到风投之后，可能第一轮融资后。我们融了 40 万美元左右。在挪威增长非常快，非常迅猛。就像有个加拿大女友一样，我的 app 在挪威很火，相信我。所以我们直接坐飞机去了挪威，然后我们就出名了。简直难以置信。你在 7-Eleven 里，人们会说：“什么？你们在 Snapchat 工作？太疯狂了。”我记得我当时想：“天哪，希望更多地方也能这样。”那是一次奇妙的经历。

turns.en: So, how did it happen?
turns.zh: 那这是怎么发生的呢？

turns.en: Well, I think one of our hypotheses is that Norway had both very high-end devices and great internet connectivity that was affordable for the population. If you think about Snapchat back in the day, you really needed an iPhone to be able to use the service. It was iPhone only in a lot of cases in the early days, and it required very heavy network use because you were sending photos and videos all the time, which was really unusual back in 2012 or whatever it was. So the kindling was dry enough for the Snap fire to really take hold. And then over time it grew in the US, but the US didn't necessarily have the same network capability or even iPhone distribution at that time.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们的一个假设是，挪威既有高端设备，又有价格合理的高速互联网。回想当年的 Snapchat，你真的需要一部 iPhone 才能用。早期很多情况下只有 iPhone 能用，而且网络使用量非常大，因为你一直在发照片和视频，这在 2012 年左右是非常不寻常的。所以引火物足够干燥，Snap 之火才能燎原。后来它在美国也发展起来了，但当时美国不一定有同样的网络能力，甚至 iPhone 的普及率也不够。

turns.en: Yes. Well, that's so interesting. How about today? Are there any countries that are fun? Norway is still the leader. It's unbelievable.
turns.zh: 是的。这太有趣了。那现在呢？有没有哪些国家很有趣？挪威仍然是领先者。真不可思议。

turns.en: Yeah. And the other thing that's interesting about Norway, and I think this is true about Snapchat in general, is it's a culture that really prioritizes close family and friend relationships. That's something that's really important to them.
turns.zh: 是的。挪威另一个有趣的地方——我认为这对 Snapchat 整体也适用——是它非常重视亲密的家庭和朋友关系。这对他们来说非常重要。

turns.en: My Norway impression was the infrastructure is so good. This country is so rich. I think they build infrastructure beyond what's sensible. You'll be driving to a village that has a population of 200 people and you'll pass through 7 km of underground tunnels to get there when anything else would be a ferry. And the person pouring you a Guinness has a graduate degree.
turns.zh: 我对挪威的印象是基础设施太好了。这个国家太富了。我觉得他们建基础设施超出了合理的程度。你开车去一个只有 200 人的村庄，会经过 7 公里的地下隧道，而其他方式只能坐渡轮。给你倒吉尼斯黑啤的人都有研究生学历。

turns.en: Exactly. Yeah. No, it's a funny country.
turns.zh: 完全正确。是啊，真是个有趣的国家。

turns.en: How has your leadership style changed over the years at Snap?
turns.zh: 你在 Snap 这些年的领导风格发生了怎样的变化？

turns.en: In so many profound ways. I think everything about leadership is about people. I love our people, but I didn't start learning how to lead people. I started building products. So I think the transition from trying to make stuff that people really love to trying to lead in a way that can inspire people is really hard. It's really hard to do what we're doing: competing against the largest companies in the world in the messaging space, building hardware, again competing with the largest companies in the world, working on foundational models. When I look at our video models and everything we're doing, we're trying to do some of the hardest things in the world. That requires inspiring people to take on enormous challenges, recruiting some of the best folks in the world to work together with us, and then building a culture where those people can do the absolute best work of their lives. That's different in many ways than just trying to build a product that people love. But it's the same in that it requires a lot of empathy and understanding people, understanding that people are very different, understanding how to bring out the best in each of them. That's one of the challenges I love most about the job today.
turns.zh: 在很多深刻的方面。我认为领导力的一切都关乎人。我爱我们的人，但我一开始并不是学怎么领导人的，我是做产品的。所以从努力做出人们喜欢的东西，到以能激励人的方式去领导，这个转变非常难。我们做的事很难：在消息领域与全球最大的公司竞争，做硬件，再次与全球最大的公司竞争，研究基础模型。看看我们的视频模型和所有工作，我们在尝试做世界上最难的事情之一。这需要激励人们迎接巨大挑战，招募世界上最优秀的人才与我们合作，然后建立一种文化，让这些人能做出他们一生中最好的工作。这在很多方面不同于仅仅做出人们喜欢的产品。但相同的是，它需要大量的同理心，理解人，理解每个人都非常不同，懂得如何激发出每个人最好的一面。这是如今这份工作我最喜欢的挑战之一。

ts.sec: Hardware supply chain and US manufacturing

turns.en: In the context of building hardware, one thing I hear from people who do it is that it's harder in the US because of the supply chain dynamics. In particular, if you're making stuff in Shenzhen, there's a company just down the road that can give you the V1 overnight and then you say, "Oh, we want to change this component," and they'll swap out the production line of the component again overnight. Have you found that supply chain speed dynamic in the US a factor, where in particular as we talk about reshoring so much manufacturing, the limiting factor with US production is often how fast you can iterate essentially?
turns.zh: 在做硬件的语境下，我听做硬件的人说，在美国更难，因为供应链动态不同。特别是在深圳，附近就有一家公司可以一夜之间给你做出 V1 版本，然后你说“我们想换这个组件”，他们又能一夜之间换掉组件生产线。你是否发现美国供应链的速度是一个因素？尤其是在我们讨论将大量制造业回流时，美国生产的限制因素往往是你迭代的速度？

turns.en: But I think people are always stunned to hear that we build some of the most important components for Spectacles in the US and in the UK. We've really thought about building some of the most IP-sensitive components, some of the most sophisticated components in the US and the UK, where we can sit them directly next to R&D and engineering so we can have those really fast cycle times and iterations.
turns.zh: 但我觉得人们总是很惊讶地听说，我们在美国和英国制造 Spectacles 的一些最重要组件。我们确实考虑过，将一些最敏感的知识产权组件、最复杂的组件放在美国和英国制造，这样我们可以把它们直接放在研发和工程旁边，从而获得非常快的周期时间和迭代。

turns.en: In house or in the US at contract manufacturers?
turns.zh: 是在内部还是在美国的合同制造商那里？

turns.en: Yes. And that's really allowed us to lead on the display technology.
turns.zh: 是的。这让我们在显示技术上真正领先。

turns.en: I mean, even if you look at specs today, the resolution and performance of our waveguides far, far exceeds our competitors. And again, we have a lot of foundational IP and patents in this area. That is one thing I love about hardware. It's way easier to protect our innovations and ideas compared to software, especially today in the age of AI.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，即使你看今天的规格，我们波导的分辨率和性能也远远超过竞争对手。而且，我们在这方面拥有大量基础知识产权和专利。这是我喜欢硬件的一点。相比软件，尤其是在当今 AI 时代，保护我们的创新和想法要容易得多。

turns.en: Yeah. I think that is a huge factor these days as well. Yeah, for sure. Should we try playing a game of darts? Let's do it.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为这在当今也是一个重要因素。没错。我们要不要试试玩飞镖？来吧。

turns.en: Oh yeah, I want to try darts. That is perfect for here. We're going to have to like get up and...
turns.zh: 哦，是的，我想试试飞镖。这里太合适了。我们得站起来……

turns.en: Yeah, we have a real dart board, but we can...
turns.zh: 是的，我们有一个真正的飞镖靶，但我们可以……

turns.en: Yeah, let's... Is Satcha really number one on the darts?
turns.zh: 是的，我们……Satcha 真的是飞镖第一名吗？

turns.en: Yes. Now, we haven't played with everyone. If you want to have a go at Sasha's record, we can practice first with Special.
turns.zh: 是的。不过我们还没和所有人比过。如果你想挑战 Sasha 的记录，我们可以先用 Special 练习一下。

turns.en: Exactly. Yeah.
turns.zh: 没错。是的。

turns.en: Here you go.
turns.zh: 给你。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: And then dart should be right here. Just pick them up.
turns.zh: 然后飞镖应该就在这里。拿起来就行。

turns.en: Yeah. Oh, there we go. Oh, this is really funny. Right next to the actual... Okay, I got 28. That was a buzzer beater.
turns.zh: 是的。哦，好了。哦，这真有趣。就在实际靶子旁边……好吧，我得了 28 分。那是压哨球。

turns.en: Oh wow. Player two wins. Oh my god. Nice. 51 too. You want to play? Sure. All right. Do you see the trust?
turns.zh: 哦，哇。玩家二赢了。哦，天哪。不错。51 分也是。你想玩吗？当然。好的。你看到信任了吗？

turns.en: Yeah, I do. This is the part. That's awesome.
turns.zh: 是的，我看到了。就是这部分。太棒了。

turns.en: There you go.
turns.zh: 好了。

turns.en: This is pretty funny. Playing chess in the pub.
turns.zh: 这真有趣。在酒吧下棋。

turns.en: Uh-oh.
turns.zh: 哎呀。

turns.en: Chuck.
turns.zh: Chuck。

turns.en: I'm not going to lie, you're in a lot of trouble. Oh, wow. Nice.
turns.zh: 说实话，你麻烦大了。哦，哇。不错。

turns.en: This is awesome.
turns.zh: 这太棒了。

turns.en: Yeah, it was fun.
turns.zh: 是的，很有趣。

turns.en: See you soon.
turns.zh: 回头见。

consensus.en: Snapchat's core is messaging with close friends, not a large social network.
consensus.zh: Snapchat 的核心是与亲密朋友的消息传递，而非大型社交网络。

consensus.en: Specs glasses aim to blend wearability of normal glasses with spatial computing capability.
consensus.zh: Spectacles 眼镜旨在融合普通眼镜的可穿戴性与空间计算能力。

consensus.en: AI is transforming software development at Snap, with over two-thirds of new code written by AI.
consensus.zh: AI 正在改变 Snap 的软件开发，超过三分之二的新代码由 AI 编写。

consensus.en: Snapchat differentiates by focusing on privacy, ephemerality, and authentic communication.
consensus.zh: Snapchat 通过关注隐私、短暂性和真实沟通来差异化。

consensus.en: Distribution is increasingly valuable as AI makes software creation easier.
consensus.zh: 随着 AI 使软件创建更容易，分发变得越来越有价值。

contrarian.en: The killer app concept is a mirage; platforms should enable many bespoke use cases.
contrarian.zh: 杀手级应用的概念是海市蜃楼；平台应支持许多定制用例。

contrarian.en: Smartphones will not be replaced; they will remain the most important legacy device.
contrarian.zh: 智能手机不会被取代；它们将保持最重要的传统设备地位。

contrarian.en: Social networks suffer from anti-network effects beyond a small group of close friends.
contrarian.zh: 社交网络在亲密朋友小群体之外会遭受反网络效应。

contrarian.en: Camera glasses without a platform are not 10x better than phones, so not worth pursuing.
contrarian.zh: 没有平台的相机眼镜不比手机好 10 倍，因此不值得追求。

contrarian.en: Content moderation is more important than algorithm design for safety.
contrarian.zh: 内容审核比算法设计对安全性更重要。

contrarian.en: Snapchat has positive well-being effects, unlike other social media, per independent studies.
contrarian.zh: 根据独立研究，Snapchat 对幸福感有积极影响，不同于其他社交媒体。
